title: Megalastrum lanuginosum
intro: Megalastrum lanuginosum is a species of fern in the family Lomariopsidaceae, native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including inflammation, fever, and digestive issues, due to its potential health benefits such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. Bioactive constituents of Megalastrum lanuginosum include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are thought to be responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, often used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, as well as skin conditions, like eczema and acne.
taxonomy: Megalastrum lanuginosum belongs to the family Ophioglossaceae, commonly known as the adderstongue fern family. This family is a group of ferns characterized by their leafless or nearly leafless stems and specialized fronds. The genus Megalastrum is a part of this family and consists of species that are mainly found in tropical and subtropical regions. The classification of Megalastrum lanuginosum places it in the division Pteridophyta, which includes all vascular plants with leaves that are usually arranged in a fern-like pattern. Further classification puts it in the class Polypodiopsida, which includes the majority of fern species.
